cards.definition: the single force that has the same effect as all the forces acting on an object combined
a diagram showing all the forces acting on an object as arrows — their size and direction show magnitude and direction
the force of gravity acting on an object (Weight = mass × gravitational field strength, W = mg)
a contact force opposing relative motion between surfaces — produces heat and slows moving objects
the constant velocity reached when the driving force equals the resistive force — no net force, no acceleration
the energy transferred when a force moves an object (Work done = Force × Distance, W = Fd)
for a balanced object, the total clockwise moments equal the total anticlockwise moments
force per unit area (Pressure = Force ÷ Area) — measured in pascals (Pa)
the extension of a spring is proportional to the force applied, provided the elastic limit is not exceeded (F = ke)
the point beyond which a material will not return to its original shape after a force is removed
the rate of flow of electric charge (Current = Charge ÷ Time, I = Q/t) — measured in amperes (A)
the energy transferred per unit charge (V = W/Q) — measured in volts (V)
opposition to the flow of current (Resistance = Voltage ÷ Current, R = V/I) — measured in ohms (Ω)
a circuit where components are connected in a single loop — same current flows through each component
a circuit where components are connected in separate branches — voltage is the same across each branch
a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences a force — the basis of electric motors
generating a voltage by moving a conductor through a magnetic field — the basis of generators and transformers
a device using electromagnetic induction to change the voltage of an alternating current — step-up increases voltage, step-down decreases it
the system of cables and transformers distributing electricity across the UK — high voltage reduces energy loss during transmission
DC flows in one direction (batteries); AC reverses direction periodically (mains electricity, 50 Hz in the UK)
